item.text: I’ll inspect the finding and the affected code first, then patch only the local source needed to remove the vulnerability while preserving behavior.

item.text: The finding points at cron task authorization and service sentinel permission paths. I’m reading those areas with surrounding model/controller usage so the fix lines up with existing ownership behavior.

The controller already checks cron ownership for direct manual triggers. The risky path appears to be alarm-triggered tasks: service status reports can cause cron execution, so the remediation needs to ensure the reporting server and target service are allowed to trigger only tasks owned by the same user or an admin-scoped task.

There are existing security regression tests around cron ownership, which is useful. I’m checking whether they cover this specific service-report path or whether the production code diverged from the expected constraints.

item.text: The generic list wrapper filters list responses by `HasPermission`, so the listed `GetSortedList` calls are not themselves leaking. The remaining weak spot is the asynchronous execution side, especially where service reports are trusted to invoke trigger tasks.

item.aggregated_output: /bin/bash: line 1: go: command not found

item.text: The local container does not have `go` installed, so I can’t run the Go test suite here. I’ll still add focused regression coverage and keep the patch small enough to audit directly.

The missing check is more general than task execution: `worker` accepts a service result by service ID alone. A reporter can submit status for a service it should not monitor, which can affect history, notifications, and trigger decisions, so I’m adding the same coverage/ownership authorization on ingest.

item.text: The dispatcher already has a `canSendTaskToServer` gate. I’m going to mirror that gate in the report consumer inside `service/singleton`, because reports are a separate trust boundary from dispatch.

I’m making two scoped changes: reject unauthorized service reports before any state/history/notification work, and make cron trigger dispatch independently verify that the trigger owner is allowed to use the reporting server. That keeps the protection local to the vulnerable execution paths.

The cron trigger path now refuses a trigger when the supplied owner cannot administer or own the reporting server. Next I’m adding the service-side report authorization helper and wiring it into the worker before metrics are accepted.

The service worker now rejects reports from servers that would not have received that service task under the dispatcher’s rules. I’m adding regression tests for both coverage and cross-owner cases, plus the cron dispatch owner/server guard.
The regression tests are in place. I’m checking formatting and the resulting diff now; if the Go toolchain is unavailable here too, I’ll report that explicitly.

item.text:
Implemented the authorization remediation locally.

Changed:
- [service/singleton/servicesentinel.go](/work/service/singleton/servicesentinel.go): service monitor reports are now rejected unless the reporter server is covered by the service and owned by the service owner, or the service owner is admin.
- [service/singleton/crontask.go](/work/service/singleton/crontask.go): trigger-task dispatch now verifies the trigger owner can use the reporting server before executing trigger tasks.
- [service/singleton/security_regression_test.go](/work/service/singleton/security_regression_test.go): added regression tests for unauthorized report/trigger paths.

Verification:
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Could not run `gofmt` or `go test` because this environment does not have `gofmt`/`go` installed.
